title: The Neckan
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In summer, on the headlands,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Baltic Sea along,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sits Neckan with his harp of gold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sings his plaintive song.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Green rolls beneath the headlands,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Green rolls the Baltic Sea.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And there, below the Neckanן¿½s feet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His wife and children be.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sings not of the ocean,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Its shells and roses pale.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of earth, of earth the Neckan sings;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He hath no other tale.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sits upon the headlands,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sings a mournful stave
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of all he saw and felt on earth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Far from the green sea wave.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sings how, a knight, he wanderן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By castle, field, and town.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But earthly knights have harder hearts
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than the Sea Children own.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sings of his earthly bridal
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Priest, knights, and ladies gay.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And who art thou,ן¿½ the priest began,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sir Knight, who weddן¿½st to-day?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I am no knight,ן¿½ he answerן¿½d;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From the sea waves I come.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The knights drew sword, the ladies screamן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The surplicן¿½d priest stood dumb.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sings how from the chapel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He vanishן¿½d with his bride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bore her down to the sea halls,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beneath the cold sea tide.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sings how she sits weeping
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mid shells that round her lie.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½False Neckan shares my bed,ן¿½ she weeps;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No Christian mate have I.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sings how through the billows
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He rose to earth again,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sought a priest to sign the cross,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Neckan Heaven might gain.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sings how, on an evening,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beneath the birch trees cool,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sate and play d his harp of gold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beside the river pool.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beside the pool sate Neckan
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tears fillן¿½d his cold blue eye.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On his white mule, across the bridge,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A cassockן¿½d priest rode by.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why sittן¿½st thou there, O Neckan,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And playן¿½st thy harp of gold?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sooner shall this my staff bear leaves,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than thou shalt Heaven behold.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, lo, the staff, it budded!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It greenן¿½d, it branchן¿½d, it waved.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O ruth of God,ן¿½ the preist cried out,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This lost sea-creature saved!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The cassockן¿½d priest rode onwards,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And vanishן¿½d with his mule.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Neckan in the twilight grey
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wept by the river pool.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He wept: ן¿½The earth hath kindness,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sea, the starry poles;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Earth, sea, and sky, and God above
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, ah, not human souls!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In summer, on the headlands,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Baltic Sea along,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sits Neckan with his harp of gold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sings this plaintive song.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Matthew Arnold represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Matthew Arnold's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
